chapters.part: Groundwork
chapters.title: What even is "software"?
chapters.lead: You've used software your whole life. This chapter asks the question most courses skip: what actually is it, physically and conceptually?
chapters.html:
In short

Software is a precise instruction list for hardware. Source code (human-readable) gets translated into instructions the machine executes.

Groundwork · Chapter 00

What even is "software"?

You've used software your whole life. This chapter asks the question most courses skip: what actually is it, physically and conceptually?

A computer, at the hardware level, only ever does one kind of thing: move and manipulate numbers stored as electrical signals. Software is a precise, exhaustive list of instructions, ultimately reducible to those same numbers, that tells the hardware exactly what sequence of tiny operations to perform. A word processor, a food delivery app, and a video game are all, underneath everything, just very long, very specific instruction lists.

"Source code" is the human-readable version a developer writes, in a programming language like Python or JavaScript. Something (a compiler or interpreter, covered in chapter 02) turns that into instructions the actual hardware can carry out. Everything you'll learn across this whole Developer track lives somewhere along this pipeline.

A plain-language version
A recipe written in English is source code: precise, but not something a stove understands on its own. A chef reading and following it step by step, at the exact moment of cooking, is closer to an interpreter. A recipe translated in advance into a fully automated robot-arm program, ready to run without a human present, is closer to a compiler. Either way, "the recipe" (the software) and "the cooking" (running it) are two separate things.
Common trip-ups
  • Thinking "software" means "an app icon." The operating system itself, the tiny script formatting your file names, and the massive banking system processing your card, all equally "software."
  • Assuming code and the running program are the same thing. Source code is a static file sitting on disk; a running program is that code actually executing, with its own memory and state, right now.
0.1

Recap

Software is an exact instruction list for hardware, written first as human-readable source code, then translated into a form the machine can actually execute. This course exists to make every step of that pipeline, and everything built on top of it, make sense.

Easy
At the most basic level, what is software?
Medium
What is the difference between "source code" and a "running program"?

chapters.part: Groundwork
chapters.title: Your toolkit: terminal, editor, and Git
chapters.navTitle: Your toolkit: terminal, editor, and Git
chapters.lead: Before any language-specific course, three tools show up in literally every developer's daily work, regardless of what they build.
chapters.html:
In short

A code editor, the terminal, and Git are the three tools that show up across virtually all software development, regardless of language.

Groundwork · Chapter 0b

Your toolkit: terminal, editor, and Git

Before any language-specific course, three tools show up in literally every developer's daily work, regardless of what they build.

The three constants
A code editorwhere you actually write source code (VS Code is the most common today)
The terminal / command linea text-based way to run programs and manage files directly, covered in depth in chapter 04
Gitversion controlA tool that tracks changes to files over time, so people can collaborate without overwriting each other.
press Enter or click to read more, covered in depth across Part II

You do not need to master any of these before starting. This whole course is built so each gets introduced properly, with real depth, exactly where it becomes relevant, rather than as an intimidating wall up front.

Common trip-ups
  • Feeling like you need to "learn the terminal" fully before writing any code. Most developers pick it up gradually, command by command, as a real need for each one comes up.
  • Skipping Git because "it's just for teams." Even solo, Git gives you an undo history far more powerful than your editor's built-in undo.
0.1

Recap

A code editor, a terminal, and Git are the three tools that show up regardless of language or framework. Each gets its own proper introduction later in this course; nothing here needs to be mastered up front.

Easy
Which of these three tools is specific to only some kinds of programming, and which apply to virtually all of it?

chapters.part: Groundwork
chapters.title: Meet the project
chapters.lead: Every course on CareerLadder uses the same running example: TastyGo, a food delivery app connecting customers to restaurants through orders. Here's where it's headed in the Developer track.
chapters.html:
In short

This whole track builds toward one capstone: building, containerizing, and deploying a real slice of TastyGo's backend.

Groundwork · Chapter 0i

Meet the project

Every course on CareerLadder uses the same running example: TastyGo, a food delivery app connecting customers to restaurants through orders. Here's where it's headed in the Developer track.

SQLingo taught you to query TastyGo's data. The Business Analyst track taught you to define TastyGo's requirements. The QA track taught you to test TastyGo. This Developer track's Capstone Project has you actually build a working slice of TastyGo's backend, yourself, from scratch: an API that lets a customer place an order, containerized, sitting behind a real reverse proxy, deployed like a real application.

The road from here to that capstone
This course (Fundamentals)the concepts and vocabulary everything else builds on
Pythonthe language you'll actually write TastyGo's backend in
Django or FastAPIthe framework used to build TastyGo's actual web application or API
DevOpscontainerizing it, putting a reverse proxy in front, and deploying it for real
0.1

Recap

This entire Developer track builds toward one concrete outcome: a real, working, deployed slice of TastyGo you built yourself. Every course between here and the capstone exists to get you the specific skill needed for the next step.

Easy
What is the ultimate goal of the Capstone Project at the end of this Developer track?

chapters.part: Part I · How computers run your code
chapters.title: Programs, processes, and the operating system
chapters.navTitle: Programs, processes, and the operating system
chapters.lead: "Program," "processA running instance of a program, with its own memory.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
Program = dormant file. Process = that program actually running, with its own memory. The OS manages every process and shares hardware between them.
Part I · Chapter 01

Programs, processes, and the operating system

"Program," "processA running instance of a program, with its own memory.
press Enter or click to read more," and "operating system" get used loosely. Precisely defined, they explain a lot about how your computer actually behaves.

Three distinct things
A programa file sitting on disk, not currently doing anything
A processthat same program, actually running, with its own private memory and state
The operating systemthe software (Windows, macOS, Linux) that manages every process, shares the CPU between them, and controls access to hardware

Open two browser windows and you have two separate processes of the same program, each with its own memory, each unaware of the other's state unless they deliberately communicate. The operating system is what decides which process gets a slice of CPU time next, and cleans up a process's memory once it exits.

Common trip-ups
  • Assuming "running the same program twice" shares memory. Each process usually gets its own isolated memory unless it deliberately opts into sharing.
  • Confusing "program" (the file) with "process" (the running instance). A single program file can be the source of many simultaneous processes.
1.1

Recap

A program is a dormant file; a process is that program actually running, with its own memory; the operating system manages every process and mediates access to shared hardware like the CPU.

Easy
What is the key difference between a "program" and a "process"?
Medium
If you open the same application twice, what happens?

chapters.part: Part I · How computers run your code
chapters.title: Source code, compilers, and interpreters
chapters.lead: Every programming language uses one of two broad strategies (or a hybrid) to turn your source code into something that actually runs.
chapters.html:
In short
Compiled languages translate all code upfront, then run it separately. Interpreted languages (like Python) read and run line by line.
Part I · Chapter 02

Source code, compilers, and interpreters

Every programming language uses one of two broad strategies (or a hybrid) to turn your source code into something that actually runs.

A compilerTranslates all of your source code into a lower-level form before it ever runs.
press Enter or click to read more translates your entire program upfront, catching many mistakes before it ever runs, but requiring a separate build step every time you change something. An interpreterReads and runs your source code directly, line by line.
press Enter or click to read more reads and executes as it goes, which makes trying things out faster, but means some mistakes only surface once execution actually reaches that specific line.

Python, the language you'll learn next in this track, is interpreted, which is exactly why you can open a Python shell and try a line of code immediately, with no separate compile step first.

Common trip-ups
  • Assuming compiled languages are always "better." Compiled code often runs faster once built, but interpreted languages are usually faster to write and experiment with.
  • Believing an interpreted language has "no errors caught early." Many modern interpreters still catch obvious syntax mistakes before running anything.
2.1

Recap

Compiled languages translate the whole program upfront, then run it separately. Interpreted languages read and execute as they go. Neither is strictly better, they trade off differently between upfront safety and iteration speed.

Easy
Why can you type a line of Python into a shell and see it run immediately, with no separate build step?

chapters.part: Part I · How computers run your code
chapters.title: Variables, memory, and data types
chapters.lead: Before touching any specific language, it helps to understand what a "variable" actually is underneath the syntax.
chapters.html:
In short
A variable is a name pointing at a value in memory. Data types (number, string, boolean, list) determine what operations actually mean.
Part I · Chapter 03

Variables, memory, and data types

Before touching any specific language, it helps to understand what a "variable" actually is underneath the syntax.

A variable is a named label pointing at a specific location in memory where a value is stored. Writing customer_name = "Aarav" doesn't create a magical box called customer_name, it reserves some memory, stores the text "Aarav" there, and lets you refer to that memory location by a readable name instead of a raw address.

Common data types you'll meet across every language
Integer / numbera whole number or decimal, like an order's item count or total price
Stringtext, like a customer's name or a restaurant's address
Booleantrue or false, like whether an order has been delivered
List / collectionan ordered group of values, like all items in one order

Types matter because the same operation can mean different things depending on type: adding two numbers (2 + 2) gives 4, but "adding" two pieces of text often means joining them together, not arithmetic. Mixing up types is one of the most common sources of early bugs.

Common trip-ups
  • Treating a number stored as text ("42") as if it were the actual number 42. Depending on the language, adding "42" + "8" might give "428", not 50.
  • Assuming a variable "is" its value permanently. A variable is just a current label pointing at a value; reassigning it points the same name at something new entirely.
3.1

Recap

A variable is a name pointing at a value stored in memory. Data types (numbers, text, booleans, collections) determine what operations actually mean for that value, and mixing types up is a very common source of bugs.

Easy
What does a variable actually represent underneath the syntax?
Medium
Why might "42" + "8" not equal 50 in some languages?

chapters.part: Part I · How computers run your code
chapters.title: The command line: your most important tool
chapters.navTitle: The command line: your most important tool
chapters.lead: The terminal looks intimidating mainly because it's unfamiliar, not because it's actually complex. A handful of concepts cover almost everything you'll do with it early on.
chapters.html:
In short
Terminal commands follow: command argument --flag. E.g. ls -la = command "ls", flag "-la".
Part I · Chapter 04

The command line: your most important tool

The terminal looks intimidating mainly because it's unfamiliar, not because it's actually complex. A handful of concepts cover almost everything you'll do with it early on.

The core idea
Commandthe name of the program you want to run, e.g. ls, git, python
Argumentsextra words telling the command what to act on, e.g. cd projects ("cd" the command, "projects" the argument)
Flags/optionsextra settings, usually starting with a dash, e.g. ls -la ("show me everything, in a detailed list")

Almost every terminal command follows the same shape: command argument --flag. You are simply telling the operating system, in text, exactly what you'd otherwise do by clicking through folders and menus, just more precisely and far more repeatably.

Common trip-ups
  • Memorizing commands instead of understanding the shape. Once "command, argument, flag" clicks, unfamiliar commands become much less intimidating to guess at from documentation.
  • Fearing that one wrong command will "break everything." Most everyday commands (moving around folders, listing files, running a program) are completely safe; only a small number (like deleting files) genuinely need caution.
4.1

Recap

Terminal commands follow a consistent shape: a command, optional arguments telling it what to act on, and optional flags adjusting its behaviour. This shape, once internalized, makes new commands far less intimidating.

Easy
In the command "ls -la", what is "-la"?

chapters.part: Part II · Version control & collaboration
chapters.title: What is Git, really?
chapters.lead: GitA tool that tracks changes to files over time, so people can collaborate without overwriting each other.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
Git tracks deliberate snapshots (commits) of your project, and lets you branch off to work separately without disturbing the main history.
Part II · Chapter 05

What is Git, really?

GitA tool that tracks changes to files over time, so people can collaborate without overwriting each other.
press Enter or click to read more is often taught as a list of commands to memorize. Understanding what it's actually doing underneath makes those commands click.

Git keeps a history of snapshots of your entire project folder. Every time you "commit," you're telling Git "remember exactly what every file looks like right now, under this description." Unlike a single "undo" button, you can jump back to any past snapshot, compare any two snapshots, or branch off from one to try something risky without touching anything else.

Three core Git concepts
Repository (repo)a project folder that Git is tracking the history of
Commita saved snapshot of the whole project at one point in time, with a message describing it
Brancha separate line of commits, letting you work on something without affecting the main version
Common trip-ups
  • Treating Git like cloud backup (like Dropbox). Git only remembers what you explicitly commit; it does not automatically save every keystroke.
  • Fearing that using Git "wrong" will lose work permanently. Almost every Git mistake is recoverable, since old commits are rarely truly deleted.
5.1

Recap

Git tracks a history of deliberate snapshots (commits) of your project, and lets you branch off to work on something separately without disturbing the main line of history.

Easy
What does a Git "commit" actually represent?

chapters.part: Part II · Version control & collaboration
chapters.title: Commits, branches, and merges
chapters.lead: A visual picture of how commits and branches actually relate makes the whole model click far faster than memorizing commands.
chapters.html:
In short
A branch is a lightweight, movable pointer to a commit. Merging brings one branch's changes into another.
Part II · Chapter 06

Commits, branches, and merges

A visual picture of how commits and branches actually relate makes the whole model click far faster than memorizing commands.

A branch is just a movable label pointing at a specific commit. Creating one costs almost nothing, it's simply a new pointer. Working on a feature branch keeps your in-progress, possibly-broken changes completely separate from main until you're ready to merge them back in with a merge commit.

Common trip-ups
  • Doing all work directly on main. This means an in-progress, broken change is visible to (and could be pulled by) everyone else immediately.
  • Thinking branches are expensive or slow to create. A branch is just a lightweight pointer, creating dozens costs almost nothing.
6.1

Recap

Commits form a chain of snapshots. A branch is a movable pointer letting you diverge from that chain safely. Merging brings a branch's changes back into another, usually main.

Easy
What is a Git branch, underneath the concept?
Medium
You want to try a risky change to TastyGo's checkout page without risking the working version everyone else uses. What Git workflow would you follow?

chapters.part: Part II · Version control & collaboration
chapters.title: Working with GitHub: remotes, PRs, code review
chapters.navTitle: Working with GitHub: remotes, PRs, code review
chapters.lead: Git itself works entirely on your own machine. GitHub adds a shared, hosted copy plus tools for reviewing and merging others' changes safely.
chapters.html:
In short
A remote is a hosted copy of your repo (e.g. on GitHub). A pull request proposes a merge and invites review before it happens.
Part II · Chapter 07

Working with GitHub: remotes, PRs, code review

Git itself works entirely on your own machine. GitHub adds a shared, hosted copy plus tools for reviewing and merging others' changes safely.

Key vocabulary
Remotea copy of the repository hosted elsewhere (like GitHub), that you push to and pull from
Pull request (PR)a request to merge your branch's changes into another branch, opened for discussion first
Code reviewteammates reading your proposed changes, leaving comments, before it gets merged

A pull request is not the merge itself, it's a proposal: "here's what I changed, here's why, please look before this becomes part of main." This is exactly the checkpoint that catches bugs, style issues, or missed edge cases before they reach everyone else, similar in spirit to the QA gates you'd have learned about in the QA track.

Common trip-ups
  • Confusing "pushing to a branch" with "it's now part of main." Pushing just updates the remote copy of your branch; merging into main is a separate, deliberate step.
  • Skipping review "just this once." The whole value of a PR is a second set of eyes before a change reaches everyone; skipping it defeats that purpose.
7.1

Recap

A remote is a hosted copy of your repo. A pull request proposes merging one branch into another and opens the door for code review before that merge actually happens.

Easy
What is a pull request?

chapters.part: Part II · Version control & collaboration
chapters.title: Merge conflicts and team workflows
chapters.lead: A merge conflict sounds alarming the first time it happens. It's actually Git being careful, not broken.
chapters.html:
In short
A merge conflict happens when two branches changed the same lines differently — Git pauses and asks you to decide, rather than guessing.
Part II · Chapter 08

Merge conflicts and team workflows

A merge conflict sounds alarming the first time it happens. It's actually Git being careful, not broken.

A merge conflict happens when two branches changed the exact same lines of the exact same file in different ways, and Git genuinely cannot guess which version you want. It stops and asks you to decide, rather than silently picking one and possibly losing someone's work.

Resolving one, step by step
Git marks the conflictthe file shows both versions side by side, clearly marked
You decidekeep one version, the other, or manually combine them
You commit the resolutiontelling Git "this is what I decided, continue"
Common trip-ups
  • Panicking and force-deleting a branch to "make the conflict go away." This can discard real work; resolving the conflict directly is almost always the right move.
  • Assuming conflicts mean you did something wrong. They're a natural consequence of multiple people changing the same code, not a sign of an error.
8.1

Recap

A merge conflict happens when two branches edit the same lines differently; Git pauses and asks you to decide, rather than guessing and potentially losing work.

Easy
What causes a Git merge conflict?

chapters.part: Part III · How the internet actually works
chapters.title: The client-server model
chapters.lead: Almost everything you do online, checking TastyGo's menu, sending a message, watching a video, follows the exact same basic shape.
chapters.html:
In short
Client-server model: a client asks, a server (holding the real data/logic) answers. Underlies almost everything on the internet.
Part III · Chapter 09

The client-server model

Almost everything you do online, checking TastyGo's menu, sending a message, watching a video, follows the exact same basic shape.

A client asks for something; a serverThe protocol browsers and servers use to request and send information.
press Enter or click to read more answers. The client is usually something a person interacts with directly (an app, a browser); the server usually runs elsewhere, often always-on, waiting to answer requests from many clients at once, not just yours.

Common trip-ups
  • Assuming "server" means one specific physical box. A server is a role (something that listens and responds), which today often runs across many machines, or none you'd recognize as a traditional "computer" at all.
  • Thinking the client does the real work. For most apps, the client mainly displays things and collects input; the server holds the actual data and business logic.
9.1

Recap

The client-server model splits responsibility: a client asks, a server answers. This single shape underlies nearly everything you do on the internet.

Easy
In the client-server model, which one typically holds the actual data and business logic?

chapters.part: Part III · How the internet actually works
chapters.title: IP addresses, DNS, and domains
chapters.lead: Typing "tastygo.com" doesn't directly connect you to anything, a hidden lookup step happens first.
chapters.html:
In short
Every server has a numeric IP address. DNS translates a human-readable domain name into that address before your device can connect.
Part III · Chapter 10

IP addresses, DNS, and domains

Typing "tastygo.com" doesn't directly connect you to anything, a hidden lookup step happens first.

Every device on the internet has an IP address, a numeric identifier computers use to actually find each other. DNSTranslates a domain name like tastygo.com into the IP address computers actually use.
press Enter or click to read more is the lookup system translating a human-friendly domain name into that numeric address, so you never have to remember or type a string of numbers to reach TastyGo.

Common trip-ups
  • Assuming a domain name "is" the server. A domain name is just a friendly label; DNS maps it to whatever IP address the server actually happens to be running at, which can even change over time.
  • Not realizing DNS results are cached. Your device and network often remember a lookup for a while, which is why DNS changes can take time to "propagate" everywhere.
10.1

Recap

Every server has a numeric IP address. DNS translates human-readable domain names into that address, a lookup step that happens before your device can actually connect to anything.

Easy
What is the main job of DNS?

chapters.part: Part III · How the internet actually works
chapters.title: HTTP and HTTPS: requests, responses, status codes
chapters.navTitle: HTTP and HTTPS: requests, responses, status codes
chapters.lead: HTTPThe protocol browsers and servers use to request and send information.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
HTTP defines a standard request (method, path) and response (status code, body). HTTPS = HTTP + TLS encryption on top.
Part III · Chapter 11

HTTP and HTTPS: requests, responses, status codes

HTTPThe protocol browsers and servers use to request and send information.
press Enter or click to read more is the shared language browsers and servers use so any client can talk to any server, regardless of what either is built with.

Anatomy of an HTTP exchange
Methodwhat kind of action, e.g. GET (fetch something), POST (create/send something)
URL/pathwhat resource is being asked for, e.g. /restaurants/42
Status codea standardized signal of what happened: 200 (success), 404 (not found), 500 (server error)

HTTPS is HTTP with encryption added, so the contents of a request or response can't be read or tampered with by anyone intercepting it in between. This is exactly what a TLS certificate provides, and exactly what a reverse proxy (chapter 23) is often responsible for handling on a server's behalf.

Common trip-ups
  • Assuming HTTP and HTTPS are entirely different protocols. HTTPS is HTTP plus an encryption layer (TLS); the request/response shape underneath is the same.
  • Treating any error status the same. A 404 (client asked for something that doesn't exist) is a very different problem from a 500 (the server itself broke), worth distinguishing when debugging.
11.1

Recap

HTTP defines a standard request (method, path) and response (status code, body) shape. HTTPS adds encryption on top via TLS, so the same conversation can't be read or altered in transit.

Easy
What does HTTPS add on top of plain HTTP?

chapters.part: Part III · How the internet actually works
chapters.title: What "the cloud" actually means
chapters.lead: "The cloud" is not a magical, placeless thing. It is, very concretely, someone else's computers, that you rent instead of own.
chapters.html:
In short
"The cloud" = renting compute, storage, and networking from a provider's data centers over the internet, instead of owning hardware.
Part III · Chapter 12

What "the cloud" actually means

"The cloud" is not a magical, placeless thing. It is, very concretely, someone else's computers, that you rent instead of own.

Before cloud computing, running TastyGo would mean buying, physically housing, and maintaining your own servers. Cloud providers (AWS, Google Cloud, Azure, and others) instead let you rent computing power, storage, and networking by the hour or by usage, from data centers they own and maintain, accessible over the internet from anywhere.

What you're actually renting
Computea virtual machine or container to actually run your application on
Storagea place to keep files, database data, backups
Networkingthe ability to actually be reached over the internet, securely
Common trip-ups
  • Thinking "the cloud" means "no physical hardware anywhere." It just means hardware someone else owns and operates, that you access remotely.
  • Assuming cloud computing is automatically cheaper. It trades upfront hardware cost for ongoing usage-based billing, which can be cheaper or more expensive depending on usage patterns.
12.1

Recap

"The cloud" is renting compute, storage, and networking from a provider's data centers over the internet, instead of buying and running your own physical hardware.

Easy
What does "the cloud" fundamentally mean?

chapters.part: Part IV · The software development lifecycle
chapters.title: SDLC: from idea to maintenance
chapters.lead: The SDLCThe repeatable phases software goes through, from idea to retirement.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
SDLC: Plan → Design → Build → Test → Release → Maintain, looping back to Plan for the next cycle.
Part IV · Chapter 13

SDLC: from idea to maintenance

The SDLCThe repeatable phases software goes through, from idea to retirement.
press Enter or click to read more is the repeatable shape every piece of real software goes through, whether the team calls it that explicitly or not.

Planning defines what to build and why. Design figures out how. Building is writing the actual code. Testing (the entire focus of the QA track) confirms it works. Releasing ships it to real users. Maintaining keeps it running and fixes what breaks, feeding straight back into planning the next round.

Common trip-ups
  • Treating SDLC as a strict, one-time, top-to-bottom sequence. In practice, especially with Agile (next chapter), teams cycle through these phases repeatedly, in small loops, not once for the whole product.
  • Skipping "maintain" mentally. Most of a real application's total lifetime and cost is spent here, not in the initial build.
13.1

Recap

The SDLC's six phases, plan, design, build, test, release, maintain, describe the repeatable shape any real software project follows, looping back into planning for the next cycle.

Easy
Which SDLC phase typically takes up the most of a real application's total lifetime?

chapters.part: Part IV · The software development lifecycle
chapters.title: Waterfall vs. Agile vs. Scrum/Kanban
chapters.lead: These describe how a team moves through the SDLC's phases, not different phases themselves.
chapters.html:
In short
Waterfall = one long sequential pass. Agile = many small repeated loops (sprints), shipping and adjusting frequently. Scrum/Kanban organize Agile work.
Part IV · Chapter 14

Waterfall vs. Agile vs. Scrum/Kanban

These describe how a team moves through the SDLC's phases, not different phases themselves.

Waterfall plans everything upfront and moves through each phase once, in order, only reaching users at the very end. AgileBuilding software in small, frequent iterations with constant feedback.
press Enter or click to read more instead repeats the whole cycle in small increments (often called sprints), shipping something real every couple of weeks and adjusting based on feedback. Scrum and Kanban are two specific, popular ways of organizing Agile work day to day.

Common trip-ups
  • Assuming Agile means "no planning." Agile still plans, just in smaller, more frequent increments, adjusting as real feedback comes in rather than committing to one huge upfront plan.
  • Treating Waterfall as always wrong. For projects with genuinely fixed, well-understood requirements (some regulated or safety-critical systems), a more sequential approach can still make sense.
14.1

Recap

Waterfall moves through the SDLC once, sequentially. Agile repeats it in small increments, shipping and adjusting frequently. Scrum and Kanban are specific ways teams organize Agile work.

Easy
What is the key difference between Waterfall and Agile?

chapters.part: Part IV · The software development lifecycle
chapters.title: Where QA, BA, and DevOps fit in
chapters.navTitle: Where QA, BA, and DevOps fit in
chapters.lead: If you've taken CareerLadder's other tracks, this chapter connects them directly to the SDLC you just learned.
chapters.html:
In short
BA ≈ Planning. Developer ≈ Design/Build. QA ≈ Test (but involved earlier). DevOps ≈ Release/Maintain. Real teams collaborate across all phases.
Part IV · Chapter 15

Where QA, BA, and DevOps fit in

If you've taken CareerLadder's other tracks, this chapter connects them directly to the SDLC you just learned.

Mapping roles onto SDLC phases
Business Analystheaviest in Planning: turning a vague idea into clear, agreed requirements
Developer (you)heaviest in Design and Build: turning requirements into working code
QAheaviest in Test, but really involved from the start, reviewing requirements for testability early
DevOpsheaviest in Release and Maintain: getting code out safely and keeping it running

In practice these overlap constantly, a good developer thinks about testability while building, and a good BA stays involved well past the planning phase. But this mapping is a useful starting mental model for understanding who does what, and why they need each other.

Common trip-ups
  • Treating these as strict, siloed handoffs. "Throwing requirements over the wall" to developers, or code "over the wall" to QA, tends to produce worse software than close, continuous collaboration.
  • Assuming DevOps is just "the person who deploys stuff." Modern DevOps is as much about culture and process (automating repeatable work, catching problems early) as any specific tool.
15.1

Recap

BA, Developer, QA, and DevOps roles map loosely onto SDLC phases, planning, build, test, and release/maintain respectively, but real teams collaborate across all phases rather than working in strict silos.

Easy
Which role is typically heaviest during the Planning phase of the SDLC?

chapters.part: Part IV · The software development lifecycle
chapters.title: Dev, staging, and production environments
chapters.lead: The same code often runs in several separate environmentsA distinct copy of a system used for a specific purpose (dev, staging, production).press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
Dev (unstable, in-progress) → Staging (production-like, safe to test) → Production (the real, live system). Kept separate to protect real users.
Part IV · Chapter 16

Dev, staging, and production environments

The same code often runs in several separate environmentsA distinct copy of a system used for a specific purpose (dev, staging, production).
press Enter or click to read more, each existing for a distinct, deliberate reason.

The typical three
Development (dev)a developer's own machine or a shared unstable environment, changing constantly while building
Staging / QAa stable copy closely mirroring production, used for realistic pre-release testing
Productionthe real, live system actual customers use

Keeping these separate means a developer experimenting on their own machine can't accidentally break the real app real customers are using right now, and a nearly-final feature can be tested thoroughly on staging without any risk to production.

Common trip-ups
  • Testing directly in production "just this once." Even a small, "harmless" test can have real, customer-visible side effects.
  • Letting staging drift far out of sync with production. If staging doesn't closely resemble production (different data, different configuration), tests there stop being reliable predictors of real behaviour.
16.1

Recap

Dev, staging, and production are kept separate so risky, unfinished work never endangers the real system, and pre-release testing happens somewhere realistic but safe.

Easy
Why keep a separate staging environment instead of testing new features directly in production?

chapters.part: Part V · Databases, APIs, and the shape of an app
chapters.title: What a database actually is
chapters.lead: A database is simply a system built specifically for storing, organizing, and reliably retrieving data, far better suited to the job than a plain text file.
chapters.html:
In short
A database reliably stores, organizes, and retrieves structured data, handling consistency and concurrent access far better than a plain file.
Part V · Chapter 17

What a database actually is

A database is simply a system built specifically for storing, organizing, and reliably retrieving data, far better suited to the job than a plain text file.

This is exactly the "orders" table from SQLingo, one row per order, one column per attribute. A database engine (like PostgreSQL, MySQL, or SQLite) handles the hard parts for you: keeping data consistent when many people write at once, retrieving exactly the rows you ask for quickly even with millions of rows, and enforcing rules like "an order must belong to a real customer."

Common trip-ups
  • Thinking a database is "just a big spreadsheet." A real database engine enforces consistency and handles many simultaneous users safely, which a spreadsheet cannot.
  • Storing everything in one giant table. Splitting data into related tables (customers, restaurants, orders, as SQLingo teaches) avoids duplication and keeps data consistent.
17.1

Recap

A database is a system purpose-built for reliably storing, organizing, and retrieving structured data, handling consistency and concurrent access far better than a plain file could. SQLingo teaches how to actually query one.

Easy
What can a real database engine handle that a plain spreadsheet generally cannot?

chapters.part: Part V · Databases, APIs, and the shape of an app
chapters.title: What an API actually is
chapters.lead: An APIA defined way for one piece of software to ask another for something.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
An API is a defined contract for one piece of software to ask another for something, keeping both sides' internals independent and swappable.
Part V · Chapter 18

What an API actually is

An APIA defined way for one piece of software to ask another for something.
press Enter or click to read more is how one piece of software asks another for something, without needing to know how the other side is actually built.

When TastyGo's mobile app shows you nearby restaurants, it isn't reaching directly into a database. It sends an HTTP request to a defined API endpoint (like GET /restaurants?near=me), and the backend responds with exactly the data it agreed to provide, in an agreed format like JSON. The app never needs to know what language or database the backend actually uses underneath.

Why this separation matters
Flexibilitythe backend can change its internal implementation freely, as long as the API contract stays the same
Reusethe same API can serve a mobile app, a website, and a partner's system, all at once
Testabilityexactly what the QA track's API testing chapter is built around: testing the contract directly, independent of any UI
Common trip-ups
  • Assuming "API" only means something exposed to outside companies. Most APIs are internal, used only by your own app's own frontend talking to its own backend.
  • Confusing the API with the database directly. An API is a controlled, intentional gateway; it deliberately exposes only what it chooses to, not raw database access.
18.1

Recap

An API is a defined, agreed contract for how one piece of software can ask another for something, keeping the two sides' internal implementations independent and swappable.

Easy
Why does an API let a backend change its internal implementation freely?

chapters.part: Part V · Databases, APIs, and the shape of an app
chapters.title: Frontend, backend, and full-stack
chapters.lead: These terms describe which side of the client-server model a developer mostly works on.
chapters.html:
In short
Frontend = what the user sees/clicks. Backend = server-side logic, APIs, database. Full-stack = both. This track focuses on backend.
Part V · Chapter 19

Frontend, backend, and full-stack

These terms describe which side of the client-server model a developer mostly works on.

Frontend developers build what runs in the user's browser or app: layout, buttons, what happens when you tap something. Backend developers build what runs on the server: business rules ("can this refund be auto-approved?"), talking to the database, exposing APIs. A full-stack developer works across both. This whole Developer track focuses primarily on the backend, exactly where Python, Django, and FastAPI live.

Common trip-ups
  • Assuming frontend is "easier" than backend, or vice versa. Each has its own real complexity: frontend deals with countless devices/browsers and real-time interactivity; backend deals with data integrity, security, and scale.
  • Believing "full-stack" means equally expert at everything. Most full-stack developers still lean stronger in one direction; the label mainly means comfortable working across the boundary.
19.1

Recap

Frontend is what the user directly sees and interacts with; backend is the server-side logic and data underneath it; full-stack spans both. This Developer track focuses on backend skills.

Easy
Which layer would typically decide whether a refund should be auto-approved or routed to manual review?

chapters.part: Part V · Databases, APIs, and the shape of an app
chapters.title: Authentication vs. authorization
chapters.lead: Two words that sound almost identical, but answer genuinely different questions, and both matter every time you build anything that logs users in.
chapters.html:
In short
Authentication = who are you? Authorization = what are you allowed to do/see? Both must be enforced on the backend itself.
Part V · Chapter 20

Authentication vs. authorization

Two words that sound almost identical, but answer genuinely different questions, and both matter every time you build anything that logs users in.

Two separate questions
Authenticationwho are you? (usually: proving it via a password, an app token, or similar)
Authorizationnow that we know who you are, what are you allowed to do or see?

Logging into TastyGo proves who you are (authentication). Whether you're then allowed to view someone else's order history is a completely separate check (authorization), and a system can absolutely get one right while getting the other wrong, exactly the kind of bug the QA track's security testing chapter is built to catch.

Common trip-ups
  • Assuming being logged in means you're allowed to do anything. Being authenticated only proves identity; every specific action still needs its own authorization check.
  • Building authorization checks only in the frontend. A backend must independently verify authorization itself, since a determined user can bypass frontend-only checks entirely.
20.1

Recap

Authentication confirms identity; authorization determines permission. Both need to be enforced on the backend itself, not just assumed from a successful login.

Easy
A customer logs in successfully but then tries to view another customer's private order history. Which check should prevent this?

chapters.part: Part VI · Servers, proxies, containers, and the cloud
chapters.title: What is a "server," really?
chapters.lead: This is where the words that trip up even experienced developers, reverse proxyA server that sits in front of your app and forwards requests to it.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
"Server" = either the machine (hardware) or the software listening for and responding to requests. Keep the two meanings separate.
Part VI · Chapter 21

What is a "server," really?

This is where the words that trip up even experienced developers, reverse proxyA server that sits in front of your app and forwards requests to it.
press Enter or click to read more, containerA lightweight, isolated package with an app and everything it needs to run.
press Enter or click to read more, serverlessRunning code where the cloud provider manages the servers, and you're billed only for actual execution time.
press Enter or click to read more, actually get untangled, one at a time, starting with "server" itself.

"Server" gets used to mean at least two different things, and mixing them up is exactly why the word feels confusing: (1) a physical or virtual machine, the actual hardware (or a cloud provider's virtualized slice of hardware) something runs on, and (2) a piece of serverA program (or the machine running it) that listens for requests and responds to them.
press Enter or click to read more software running on that machine, listening for requests and responding to them. You can run several different server programs on one machine, or spread one logical service across many machines.

Common trip-ups
  • Assuming "the server" always means one physical box. A single logical "server" (like TastyGo's backend) commonly runs across many machines behind the scenes.
  • Conflating the machine with the software running on it. "The web server" almost always refers to the software (like nginx), not the physical hardware underneath it.
21.1

Recap

"Server" can mean either the machine (hardware) or the software running on it that listens for and responds to requests. Keeping these two meanings separate makes every following chapter in this Part much clearer.

Easy
What are the two different things "server" commonly refers to?

chapters.part: Part VI · Servers, proxies, containers, and the cloud
chapters.title: Web servers vs. application servers
chapters.lead: Here's where nginx specifically fits, a piece of software with a genuinely narrow, well-defined job.
chapters.html:
In short
Web server (e.g. nginx) serves static files fast and handles connections at scale. Application server actually runs your app's code/logic.
Part VI · Chapter 22

Web servers vs. application servers

Here's where nginx specifically fits, a piece of software with a genuinely narrow, well-defined job.

Two different jobs, often on the same machine
Web server (e.g. nginx, Apache)extremely good at serving static files quickly and handling huge numbers of simultaneous connections; often sits in front as a reverse proxy too
Application server (e.g. gunicorn for Python, or your Django/FastAPI app itself)actually runs your application's code: the logic that decides what a request should do

A typical real setup: nginx receives every incoming request first. Requests for static files (images, CSS) it answers directly, extremely fast. Requests that need actual logic (place an order, check a status) it forwards along to the application server running your Python/Django/FastAPI code, then relays that response back to the client.

Common trip-ups
  • Assuming you need only one or the other. Real production setups very commonly use both together, each doing what it's best at.
  • Thinking your application code "is" the web server. Your Django/FastAPI app is usually run by an application server, often sitting behind a separate web server/reverse proxy in front of it.
22.1

Recap

A web server (like nginx) excels at serving static content and handling connections at scale; an application server actually runs your code's logic. Production setups typically use both together.

Easy
In a typical setup, what happens to a request for a static image versus a request to place an order?

chapters.part: Part VI · Servers, proxies, containers, and the cloud
chapters.title: Proxies and reverse proxies, explained simply
chapters.navTitle: Proxies and reverse proxies, explained simply
chapters.lead: One of the most commonly confused pairs in all of backend development, cleared up with a single question: whose side is it standing on?
chapters.html:
In short
Forward proxy works on behalf of clients. Reverse proxy (e.g. nginx) works on behalf of servers: routing, load balancing, centralized HTTPS.
Part VI · Chapter 23

Proxies and reverse proxies, explained simply

One of the most commonly confused pairs in all of backend development, cleared up with a single question: whose side is it standing on?

A forward proxy sits in front of clients, hiding and managing their traffic out to the internet (common in corporate networks, or privacy tools). A reverse proxyA server that sits in front of your app and forwards requests to it.
press Enter or click to read more sits in front of servers instead, hiding and managing how traffic reaches them: it can route requests to whichever of several app server instances is free, handle HTTPS/TLS in one central place, and shield the actual application servers from being directly exposed to the internet.

This is precisely nginx's most common real-world role in front of a Python backend: nginx (the reverse proxy) receives every request from the internet, and only it, then decides where each request actually goes.

Common trip-ups
  • Mixing up which side each proxy protects. The simplest memory aid: a forward proxy works for the client asking; a reverse proxy works for the server(s) answering.
  • Assuming a reverse proxy is optional overhead. In production, it's usually what provides load balancing, centralized HTTPS, and hides your real app servers from direct exposure, real, valuable jobs, not just extra complexity.
23.1

Recap

A reverse proxy sits in front of one or more backend servers, routing, load balancing, and often handling HTTPS on their behalf, exactly the role nginx commonly plays in front of a real application.

Easy
What is the simplest way to remember the difference between a forward proxy and a reverse proxy?
Medium
TastyGo runs three copies of its backend for reliability. Explain, in plain terms, what role a reverse proxy plays here.

chapters.part: Part VI · Servers, proxies, containers, and the cloud
chapters.title: Containers, Docker, and serverless
chapters.lead: The last set of "words that confused you": what problem each of these actually solves, and how they relate.
chapters.html:
In short
VMs = full separate guest OS per app (heavy). Containers share the host OS kernel (light). Docker = popular container tool. Serverless = no server management, billed per execution.
Part VI · Chapter 24

Containers, Docker, and serverless

The last set of "words that confused you": what problem each of these actually solves, and how they relate.

A traditional virtual machine gives each app its own complete, separate operating system, safe but heavy, slow to start, and resource-hungry. A containerA lightweight, isolated package with an app and everything it needs to run.
press Enter or click to read more instead packages just the app and its exact dependencies, sharing the host machine's OS kernel underneath, making it far lighter and faster to start. Docker is simply the most popular tool for building, running, and sharing containers.

ServerlessRunning code where the cloud provider manages the servers, and you're billed only for actual execution time.
press Enter or click to read more takes this a step further: you don't manage a container or a server at all, you just deploy a function, and the cloud provider runs it only when triggered, scaling automatically, billing you only for actual execution time, not for sitting idle. It solves a different problem than containers: not "how do I package my app efficiently," but "how do I avoid managing any server infrastructure whatsoever" for things that don't need to run constantly.

Common trip-ups
  • Assuming Docker and containers are the same word for the same thing. "Container" is the general concept; Docker is one (very popular) specific tool for working with containers.
  • Assuming serverless means "no servers exist." Servers still run your code, you simply never provision or manage them yourself.
  • Picking serverless for something that runs constantly. Serverless shines for infrequent, bursty, or event-driven work; an always-busy backend often suits a regular container or server better, cost-wise.
24.1

Recap

Containers package an app with its dependencies, sharing the host OS kernel, lighter than a full virtual machine. Docker is the most common tool for containers. Serverless removes server management entirely, running your code on demand and billing only for actual execution.

Easy
What is the key difference between a virtual machine and a container?
Medium
When would serverless generally be a poor fit compared to a regular container or server?

chapters.part: Part VII · Building and shipping software
chapters.title: Package managers and dependencies
chapters.lead: Almost no real application is written entirely from scratch. Package managers are how you safely borrow other people's already-written code.
chapters.html:
In short
Package managers (pip, npm) install and track external dependencies. Pin exact versions so behaviour stays consistent across machines.
Part VII · Chapter 25

Package managers and dependencies

Almost no real application is written entirely from scratch. Package managers are how you safely borrow other people's already-written code.

A dependency is a piece of external code your project relies on, someone else's library that already solves a problem (parsing dates, sending emails, talking to a database) so you don't have to write it yourself. A package manager (like pip for Python, or npm for JavaScript) downloads, installs, and tracks exactly which versions of which dependencies your project needs.

Why versions matter
Version pinningrecording the exact version used, so the project behaves identically on every machine it runs on
Updatingdeliberately moving to a newer version, often for security fixes, ideally tested before deploying
Common trip-ups
  • Never pinning dependency versions. Without this, "it works on my machine" can genuinely mean a teammate silently got a different, incompatible version.
  • Blindly updating every dependency to the latest version right before a release. A newer version can introduce breaking changes; updates deserve their own testing, ideally on staging first.
25.1

Recap

Package managers install and track external dependencies your code relies on. Pinning exact versions keeps a project's behaviour consistent across every machine it runs on.

Easy
Why does pinning an exact dependency version matter?

chapters.part: Part VII · Building and shipping software
chapters.title: Testing your own code
chapters.lead: The QA track covered testing an entire application from the outside. As a developer, you also write tests for your own code directly, before QA ever sees it.
chapters.html:
In short
Unit tests check one small piece of your own code in isolation, fast feedback, cheaper bugs than catching them later in QA or production.
Part VII · Chapter 26

Testing your own code

The QA track covered testing an entire application from the outside. As a developer, you also write tests for your own code directly, before QA ever sees it.

A unit test checks one small piece of your code (a single function) in isolation. For example, a function computing a delivery ETA can be tested directly with known inputs, without needing a running app, a real database, or a browser at all: give it known inputs, assert the output is exactly what's expected.

Why developers test their own code, not just QA
Speedunit tests run in milliseconds, giving instant feedback while you're still writing the code
Confidence to change thingsa good test suite tells you immediately if a later change broke something, without waiting for QA to find it
Cheaper bugsa bug caught by a unit test while writing the code is far cheaper to fix than the same bug found later by QA, or worse, by a customer
Common trip-ups
  • Treating "QA will catch it" as a substitute for writing your own tests. A bug caught earlier, by you, while writing the code, is dramatically cheaper to fix than one found later.
  • Only testing the happy path in your own tests. The same positive/negative testing principle from the QA track applies here too.
26.1

Recap

Unit tests check small pieces of your own code in isolation, giving fast feedback and making later changes far safer, complementing (not replacing) the broader testing QA does from the outside.

Easy
Why is a bug caught by a developer's own unit test generally cheaper to fix than one found later by QA?

chapters.part: Part VII · Building and shipping software
chapters.title: CI/CD, from a builder's perspective
chapters.lead: The QA track covered CI/CD as a way to catch regressions. As the person writing the code, it's also what actually gets your work safely in front of real users.
chapters.html:
In short
CI = automatically build + test every change. CD = automatically ship changes that pass. Together: fast, visible, automatic safety net.
Part VII · Chapter 27

CI/CD, from a builder's perspective

The QA track covered CI/CD as a way to catch regressions. As the person writing the code, it's also what actually gets your work safely in front of real users.

CI vs. CD
Continuous Integration (CI)every time code is pushed, it's automatically built and tested, catching problems immediately
Continuous Deployment/Delivery (CD)code that passes CI is automatically (or with one click) released toward staging or production

As a developer, this changes how you work day to day: instead of manually testing and manually copying files to a server, you push your code, an automated pipeline builds it, runs the test suite, and (if everything passes) ships it, often within minutes, with a clear, visible record of exactly what changed and whether it was verified.

Common trip-ups
  • Pushing code straight to production manually "to save time." This skips the automated safety net entirely, exactly the regressions CI/CD exists to catch.
  • Writing code with no tests, then wondering why CI doesn't catch anything. CI can only be as good as the tests it runs, chapter 26's unit tests are what actually give it teeth.
27.1

Recap

CI automatically builds and tests every code change; CD automatically (or semi-automatically) ships code that passes. Together they turn "did I break anything" into an automatic, fast, visible answer instead of a manual guess.

Easy
What is the key difference between Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment?

chapters.part: Part VIII · Where to go next
chapters.title: Choosing your path: Python, Django, FastAPI, DevOps
chapters.navTitle: Choosing your path: Python, Django, FastAPI, DevOps
chapters.lead: With the vocabulary from this course in place, here's what's actually different about each of the courses ahead.
chapters.html:
In short
Python = the shared language. Django = full-featured web framework. FastAPI = lean API-focused framework. DevOps = deploying either reliably.
Part VIII · Chapter 28

Choosing your path: Python, Django, FastAPI, DevOps

With the vocabulary from this course in place, here's what's actually different about each of the courses ahead.

What each remaining course actually teaches
Pythonthe language itself: syntax, data structures, functions, object-oriented code, the foundation everything else here is built on
Djangoa full-featured web framework: build a complete web application (pages, forms, an admin panel, a database layer) quickly, with a lot of structure provided for you
FastAPIa lean, modern framework focused specifically on building fast, well-documented APIs, less "batteries-included" than Django, more focused
DevOpstaking any of the above and actually running it reliably: containers, reverse proxies, CI/CD, cloud deployment, monitoring

You don't have to choose only one. Many real backend developers know Python plus both frameworks, reaching for Django when building a fuller web application with an admin interface, and FastAPI when building a lean, high-performance API. DevOps skills matter regardless of which framework you use.

28.1

Recap

Python is the shared foundation. Django and FastAPI are two different tools for two different kinds of backend project. DevOps skills apply to deploying either one reliably. The Capstone Project at the end brings all of this together.

Easy
What is the main practical difference between Django and FastAPI?

chapters.part: Part VIII · Where to go next
chapters.title: Career paths in software development
chapters.lead: The final chapter of Fundamentals: where developer careers actually lead, and how the vocabulary from this course keeps showing up in interviews.
chapters.html:
In short
"Walk me through typing a URL into a browser": DNS lookup → HTTP(S) request → reverse proxy → app server → (maybe) database → response back.
Part VIII · Chapter 29

Career paths in software development

The final chapter of Fundamentals: where developer careers actually lead, and how the vocabulary from this course keeps showing up in interviews.

Common paths from here
Backend developerbuilds the server-side logic, APIs, and data layer, exactly what Python/Django/FastAPI lead toward
Full-stack developerworks across both frontend and backend
DevOps / Platform engineerfocuses on deployment, infrastructure, and reliability, exactly Part VI and VII of this course, deepened

Interviewers frequently ask conceptual questions straight from this chapter list: "what's the difference between a process and a thread," "explain what happens when you type a URL into a browser" (client-server, DNS, HTTP, all covered here), "what's a reverse proxy," "why use Docker." Being able to explain these plainly, the way this course tried to, is worth as much as knowing specific syntax.

29.1

Recap

This course built the shared vocabulary and mental models, how software runs, how the internet works, the SDLC, and the servers/proxies/containers that confuse even experienced developers, that every course ahead, and most real interviews, will keep building on.

Hard
An interviewer asks: "Walk me through what happens, step by step, from typing tastygo.com into a browser to seeing the homepage." Write your own answer using concepts from this course.
Medium
Which single course in this Developer track focuses specifically on deployment, containers, and reliability at a deeper level than this Fundamentals course?

extras.title: Developer fundamentals interview questions
extras.lead: A deep, topic-by-topic bank of the "how does software actually work" questions that come up in junior developer and DevOps-adjacent interviews. Every answer is short, correct, and points at the reasoning an interviewer wants to hear. Click any question to expand it.
extras.html:
Interview prep

Developer fundamentals interview questions

A deep, topic-by-topic bank of the "how does software actually work" questions that come up in junior developer and DevOps-adjacent interviews. Every answer is short, correct, and points at the reasoning an interviewer wants to hear. Click any question to expand it.

Software & the SDLC

BeginnerWhat are the phases of the SDLC?

Planning, requirements/analysis, design, development, testing, deployment, and maintenance. Models like Waterfall run them once in sequence; Agile repeats them in short iterations.

BeginnerWaterfall vs Agile?

Waterfall does all requirements and design up front in sequential phases — predictable but rigid. Agile delivers working software in short iterations and welcomes change — adaptive but needs continuous engagement.

BeginnerCompiled vs interpreted languages?

A compiled language (C, Go, Rust) is translated to machine code ahead of time, then run — fast, errors caught at compile time. An interpreted language (Python, JavaScript) is executed line by line by a runtime — more flexible, errors often surface at run time. Many (Java, C#) compile to bytecode run by a VM.

BeginnerFrontend vs backend?

Frontend is what runs in the browser (UI, HTML/CSS/JS). Backend is the server side (business logic, database, APIs). They communicate over HTTP, usually via an API.

IntermediateWhat is an API?

An Application Programming Interface: a defined contract for how one piece of software talks to another. A web API exposes endpoints (URLs + methods) that return data (often JSON), hiding the internals behind that contract.

Version control & Git

BeginnerWhat is version control, and why Git?

It tracks changes to code over time, enabling history, collaboration, branching and rollback. Git is distributed — every clone is a full repository with its own history — which makes branching cheap and offline work easy.

BeginnerExplain the working directory, staging area and repository.

You edit files in the working directory, choose what to include with git add (the staging area), record a snapshot with git commit (the local repository), and share with git push.

Working dir—add→Staging—commit→Local repo—push→Remote
Intermediategit merge vs git rebase?

Merge combines branches and preserves history with a merge commit (non-linear but truthful). Rebase replays your commits on top of another branch for a clean, linear history. Rule of thumb: do not rebase commits that others have already pulled (rewriting shared history).

Intermediategit pull vs git fetch?

fetch downloads remote changes but does not touch your working branch; pull is fetch + merge (or rebase) into your branch. Fetch to look first; pull to integrate.

Advancedgit reset vs git revert?

revert creates a new commit that undoes a previous one — safe for shared history. reset moves the branch pointer (and optionally changes the index/working tree), rewriting history — fine locally, dangerous on pushed commits.

IntermediateWhat is a pull request, and why review code?

A PR proposes merging one branch into another and is the place for review, discussion and automated checks (CI) before merge. Review catches bugs, spreads knowledge, and keeps standards consistent.

IntermediateHow do you resolve a merge conflict?

Git marks the clashing regions; you open the file, choose the correct combination of both sides, remove the conflict markers, then git add and complete the merge/commit. Conflicts happen when two branches change the same lines.

How the web works

IntermediateWhat happens when you type a URL and press enter?

The browser resolves the domain to an IP via DNS, opens a TCP connection (and a TLS handshake for HTTPS), sends an HTTP request, the server responds, and the browser renders the returned HTML/CSS/JS (making more requests for assets).

Browser→DNS lookup→TCP + TLS→HTTP request→Server→Response→Render
BeginnerWhat is DNS?

The Domain Name System — the internet's phone book. It translates a human name like example.com into the IP address a machine needs to connect to.

BeginnerName the main HTTP methods.

GET (read), POST (create), PUT (replace), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (remove). GET should be safe and have no side effects; GET/PUT/DELETE are idempotent, POST is not.

BeginnerWhat do the HTTP status code families mean?
RangeMeaningExamples
1xxInformational100 Continue
2xxSuccess200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content
3xxRedirection301 Moved, 304 Not Modified
4xxClient error400 Bad Request, 401, 403, 404
5xxServer error500 Internal, 502, 503
IntermediateHTTP vs HTTPS — what does TLS add?

HTTPS is HTTP over TLS, which encrypts traffic (confidentiality), verifies the server via a certificate (authentication), and detects tampering (integrity). Without it, data travels in plain text.

IntermediateWhat is REST?

An architectural style for web APIs: resources identified by URLs, manipulated with standard HTTP methods, stateless requests, and representations (usually JSON). It is convention, not a strict protocol.

IntermediateHTTP is stateless — so how do sites remember you?

Each request is independent, so state is carried explicitly: a cookie holds a session id the server looks up (server-side session), or a signed token (e.g. JWT) carries identity/claims the server verifies without storing session state.

BeginnerWhat is JSON?

JavaScript Object Notation — a lightweight, human-readable text format of key/value pairs and arrays, the common language for API request/response bodies.

Servers & infrastructure

IntermediateWeb server vs application server?

A web server (nginx, Apache) serves static files and forwards requests; an application server runs your code to generate dynamic responses. In practice a web server often sits in front of the app server.

IntermediateWhat is a reverse proxy, and why use one?

A server that sits in front of your app servers and forwards client requests to them. It centralises TLS termination, load balancing, caching, compression and routing — and hides the backend. nginx is a common choice.

IntermediateWhat is a load balancer?

It distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers so no single one is overwhelmed, improving throughput and availability (it routes around unhealthy instances). Strategies include round-robin and least-connections.

IntermediateWhat is caching, and what is a CDN?

Caching stores expensive-to-produce results so repeat requests are served fast. A CDN is a network of edge servers that cache static assets close to users, cutting latency and origin load.

BeginnerWhat is a port, and what is localhost?

A port is a numbered channel on a host so multiple services can share one IP (80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS). localhost (127.0.0.1) is your own machine — used to reach services running locally.

Containers & deployment

IntermediateContainer vs virtual machine?

A VM virtualises hardware and runs a full guest OS; a container shares the host kernel and packages just the app and its dependencies — lighter, faster to start, and denser, at the cost of weaker isolation.

Virtual machineContainer
IncludesA full guest OSApp + deps only (shares host kernel)
SizeGigabytesMegabytes
Start timeMinutesSeconds
IsolationStrong (own OS)Process-level
IntermediateWhat is Docker, and image vs container?

Docker packages an app and its dependencies into a portable image (a read-only template) that runs the same everywhere. A container is a running instance of an image. "Works on my machine" problems largely disappear.

AdvancedWhat is serverless?

A model where you deploy functions and the cloud provider runs, scales and bills them per use — no servers to manage (e.g. AWS Lambda). Great for event-driven, bursty workloads; trade-offs include cold starts and vendor lock-in.

IntermediateWhat is CI/CD?

CI (continuous integration) automatically builds and tests every change so problems surface early. CD (continuous delivery/deployment) automatically releases passing changes to staging/production. Together they make small, safe, frequent releases.

BeginnerWhy have separate dev, staging and production environments?

To test changes safely before they reach users: dev for building, staging as a production-like rehearsal, production for real users. Keeping them separate prevents experiments from breaking live systems.

IntermediateWhat are environment variables and secrets?

Configuration passed to an app from its environment (e.g. database URL, API keys) rather than hard-coded — so the same build runs differently per environment. Secrets (passwords, keys) are sensitive env config kept out of source control and injected securely.

Core concepts

IntermediateSynchronous vs asynchronous?

Synchronous work blocks until it finishes; asynchronous work is started and the program continues, handling the result later (callback/promise/await). Async keeps apps responsive during slow I/O like network or disk.

AdvancedProcess vs thread?

A process is an independent program with its own memory; a thread is a unit of execution inside a process, sharing that process memory. Threads are lighter and share data easily, but shared memory needs careful synchronisation.

IntermediateWhat does an operating system do?

It manages hardware and resources for programs: scheduling the CPU, allocating memory, handling files and devices, and providing isolation and security between processes.

IntermediateMonolith vs microservices?

A monolith is one deployable application — simple to build and deploy, harder to scale and change in parts. Microservices split it into small independently deployable services — flexible and scalable, but with added network, data and operational complexity. Start simple; split when you must.

BeginnerSQL vs NoSQL, briefly?

SQL databases store structured data in related tables with a fixed schema and strong consistency. NoSQL (document, key-value, graph) trades some structure for flexible schemas and horizontal scale. Choose by data shape and access pattern.

extras.title: Cheat sheet
extras.lead: Every chapter's core idea on one page.
extras.html:
Quick reference

Cheat sheet

Every chapter's core idea on one page.

Groundwork

00 What even is "software"?
Software is a precise instruction list for hardware. Source code (human-readable) gets translated into instructions the machine executes.
0b Your toolkit: terminal, editor, and Git
A code editor, the terminal, and Git are the three tools that show up across virtually all software development, regardless of language.
0i Meet the project
This whole track builds toward one capstone: building, containerizing, and deploying a real slice of TastyGo's backend.

Part I · How computers run your code

01 Programs, processes, and the operating system
Program = dormant file. Process = that program actually running, with its own memory. The OS manages every process and shares hardware between them.
02 Source code, compilers, and interpreters
Compiled languages translate all code upfront, then run it separately. Interpreted languages (like Python) read and run line by line.
03 Variables, memory, and data types
A variable is a name pointing at a value in memory. Data types (number, string, boolean, list) determine what operations actually mean.
04 The command line: your most important tool
Terminal commands follow: command argument --flag. E.g. ls -la = command "ls", flag "-la".

Part II · Version control & collaboration

05 What is Git, really?
Git tracks deliberate snapshots (commits) of your project, and lets you branch off to work separately without disturbing the main history.
06 Commits, branches, and merges
A branch is a lightweight, movable pointer to a commit. Merging brings one branch's changes into another.
07 Working with GitHub: remotes, PRs, code review
A remote is a hosted copy of your repo (e.g. on GitHub). A pull request proposes a merge and invites review before it happens.
08 Merge conflicts and team workflows
A merge conflict happens when two branches changed the same lines differently — Git pauses and asks you to decide, rather than guessing.

Part III · How the internet actually works

09 The client-server model
Client-server model: a client asks, a server (holding the real data/logic) answers. Underlies almost everything on the internet.
10 IP addresses, DNS, and domains
Every server has a numeric IP address. DNS translates a human-readable domain name into that address before your device can connect.
11 HTTP and HTTPS: requests, responses, status codes
HTTP defines a standard request (method, path) and response (status code, body). HTTPS = HTTP + TLS encryption on top.
12 What "the cloud" actually means
"The cloud" = renting compute, storage, and networking from a provider's data centers over the internet, instead of owning hardware.

Part IV · The software development lifecycle

13 SDLC: from idea to maintenance
SDLC: Plan → Design → Build → Test → Release → Maintain, looping back to Plan for the next cycle.
14 Waterfall vs. Agile vs. Scrum/Kanban
Waterfall = one long sequential pass. Agile = many small repeated loops (sprints), shipping and adjusting frequently. Scrum/Kanban organize Agile work.
15 Where QA, BA, and DevOps fit in
BA ≈ Planning. Developer ≈ Design/Build. QA ≈ Test (but involved earlier). DevOps ≈ Release/Maintain. Real teams collaborate across all phases.
16 Dev, staging, and production environments
Dev (unstable, in-progress) → Staging (production-like, safe to test) → Production (the real, live system). Kept separate to protect real users.

Part V · Databases, APIs, and the shape of an app

17 What a database actually is
A database reliably stores, organizes, and retrieves structured data, handling consistency and concurrent access far better than a plain file.
18 What an API actually is
An API is a defined contract for one piece of software to ask another for something, keeping both sides' internals independent and swappable.
19 Frontend, backend, and full-stack
Frontend = what the user sees/clicks. Backend = server-side logic, APIs, database. Full-stack = both. This track focuses on backend.
20 Authentication vs. authorization
Authentication = who are you? Authorization = what are you allowed to do/see? Both must be enforced on the backend itself.

Part VI · Servers, proxies, containers, and the cloud

21 What is a "server," really?
"Server" = either the machine (hardware) or the software listening for and responding to requests. Keep the two meanings separate.
22 Web servers vs. application servers
Web server (e.g. nginx) serves static files fast and handles connections at scale. Application server actually runs your app's code/logic.
23 Proxies and reverse proxies, explained simply
Forward proxy works on behalf of clients. Reverse proxy (e.g. nginx) works on behalf of servers: routing, load balancing, centralized HTTPS.
24 Containers, Docker, and serverless
VMs = full separate guest OS per app (heavy). Containers share the host OS kernel (light). Docker = popular container tool. Serverless = no server management, billed per execution.

Part VII · Building and shipping software

25 Package managers and dependencies
Package managers (pip, npm) install and track external dependencies. Pin exact versions so behaviour stays consistent across machines.
26 Testing your own code
Unit tests check one small piece of your own code in isolation, fast feedback, cheaper bugs than catching them later in QA or production.
27 CI/CD, from a builder's perspective
CI = automatically build + test every change. CD = automatically ship changes that pass. Together: fast, visible, automatic safety net.

Part VIII · Where to go next

28 Choosing your path: Python, Django, FastAPI, DevOps
Python = the shared language. Django = full-featured web framework. FastAPI = lean API-focused framework. DevOps = deploying either reliably.
29 Career paths in software development
"Walk me through typing a URL into a browser": DNS lookup → HTTP(S) request → reverse proxy → app server → (maybe) database → response back.
